title: Ambient ionization mass spectrometry imaging platform for direct mapping from bulk tissue
abstract: A method of ion imaging is disclosed that includes automatically sampling a plurality of different locations on a sample (20) using a first device (21) which is arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from the sample (20). Mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data corresponding to each location is obtained and the obtained mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data is used to construct, train or improve a sample classification model.
claims:
Claims Claims 1. A method of ion imaging comprising: automatically sampling a plurality of different locations on a sample using a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from the sample; obtaining mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data corresponding to each said location; and using said obtained mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data to construct, train or improve a sample classification model. 2. A method as claimed in claim 1 , wherein said sample comprises a biological sample, biological tissue, human tissue, animal tissue, biological matter, a bacterial colony, a fungal colony or one or more bacterial strains. 3. A method as claimed in claim 1 or 2, wherein said sample comprises native or unmodified sample material. 4. A method as claimed in claim 3, wherein said native or unmodified sample material is unmodified by the addition of a matrix or reagent. 5. A method as claimed in any of claims 2-4, wherein said biological tissue comprises in vivo biological tissue. 6. A method as claimed in any of claims 2-4, wherein said biological tissue comprises ex vivo biological tissue. 7. A method as claimed in any of claims 2-4, wherein said biological tissue comprises in vitro biological tissue. 8. A method as claimed in any of claims 2-7, wherein said biological tissue comprises either: (i) adrenal gland tissue, appendix tissue, bladder tissue, bone, bowel tissue, brain tissue, breast tissue, bronchi, coronal tissue, ear tissue, esophagus tissue, eye tissue, gall bladder tissue, genital tissue, heart tissue, hypothalamus tissue, kidney tissue, large intestine tissue, intestinal tissue, larynx tissue, liver tissue, lung tissue, lymph nodes, mouth tissue, nose tissue, pancreatic tissue, parathyroid gland tissue, pituitary gland tissue, prostate tissue, rectal tissue, salivary gland tissue, skeletal muscle tissue, skin tissue, small intestine tissue, spinal cord, spleen tissue, stomach tissue, thymus gland tissue, trachea tissue, thyroid tissue, ureter tissue, urethra tissue, soft and connective tissue, peritoneal tissue, blood vessel tissue and/or fat tissue; (ii) grade I, grade II, grade III or grade IV cancerous tissue; (iii) metastatic cancerous tissue; (iv) mixed grade cancerous tissue; (v) a 

 sub-grade cancerous tissue; (vi) healthy or normal tissue; or (vii) cancerous or abnormal tissue . 9. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said sample classification model comprises a biological sample classification model, a biological tissue classification model, a human tissue classification model, an animal tissue classification model or a bacterial strain classification model. 10. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising constructing, training or improving said sample classification model in order either: (i) to distinguish between healthy and diseased tissue; (ii) to distinguish between potentially cancerous and non-cancerous tissue; (iii) to distinguish between different types or grades of cancerous tissue; (iv) to distinguish between different types or classes of sample material; (v) to determine whether or not one or more desired or undesired substances are present in said sample; (vi) to confirm the identity or authenticity of said sample; (vii) to determine whether or not one or more impurities, illegal substances or undesired substances are present in said sample; (viii) to determine whether a human or animal patient is at an increased risk of suffering an adverse outcome; (ix) to make or assist in the making a diagnosis or prognosis; and (x) to inform a surgeon, nurse, medic or robot of a medical, surgical or diagnostic outcome. 1 1. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein the step of using said obtained mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data to construct, train or improve said sample classification model comprises performing a supervised or unsupervised multivariate statistical analysis of said mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data. 12. A method as claimed in claim 1 1 , wherein said multivariate statistical analysis is selected from the group consisting of: (i) principal component analysis ("PCA"); and (ii) linear discriminant analysis ("LDA"). 13. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising analysing a profile of said aerosol, smoke or vapour or a profile of ions derived from said aerosol, smoke or vapour. 14. A method as claimed in claim 13, wherein said profile is selected from the group consisting of: (i) a lipidomic profile; (ii) a fatty acid profile; (iii) a phospholipid profile; (iv) a phosphatidic acid (PA) profile; (v) a phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) profile; (vi) a phosphatidylglycerol (PG) profile; (vii) a phosphatidylserines (PS) profile; (viii) a phosphatidylinositol (PI) profile; or (ix) a triglyceride (TG) profile. 15. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising automatically translating said sample relative to said first device before and/or during and/or after 

 obtaining mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data from at least some of said locations on said sample. 16. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device comprises or forms part of an ambient ion or ionization source or wherein said first device generates said aerosol, smoke or vapour for subsequent ionization by an ambient ion or ionization source or other ionization source. 17. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device is arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from said sample without said sample requiring prior preparation. 18. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device comprises an ion source selected from the group consisting of: (i) a rapid evaporative ionization mass spectrometry ("REIMS") ion source; (ii) a desorption electrospray ionization ("DESI") ion source; (iii) a laser desorption ionization ("LDI") ion source; (iv) a thermal desorption ion source; (v) a laser diode thermal desorption ("LDTD") ion source; (vi) a desorption electro- flow focusing ("DEFFI") ion source; (vii) a dielectric barrier discharge ("DBD") plasma ion source; (viii) an Atmospheric Solids Analysis Probe ("ASAP") ion source; (ix) an ultrasonic assisted spray ionization ion source; (x) an easy ambient sonic-spray ionization ("EASI") ion source; (xi) a desorption atmospheric pressure photoionization ("DAPPI") ion source; (xii) a paperspray ("PS") ion source; (xiii) a jet desorption ionization ("JeDI") ion source; (xiv) a touch spray ("TS") ion source; (xv) a nano-DESI ion source; (xvi) a laser ablation electrospray ("LAESI") ion source; (xvii) a direct analysis in real time ("DART") ion source; (xviii) a probe electrospray ionization ("PESI") ion source; (xix) a solid-probe assisted electrospray ionization ("SPA-ESI") ion source; (xx) a cavitron ultrasonic surgical aspirator ("CUSA") device; (xxi) a focussed or unfocussed ultrasonic ablation device; (xxii) a microwave resonance device; and (xxiii) a pulsed plasma RF dissection device. 19. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device comprises one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips and said step of automatically sampling said plurality of different locations on said sample using said first device further comprises contacting said sample with said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips at each said location. 20. A method as claimed in claim 19, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a monopolar device. 21. A method as claimed in claim 20, further comprising providing a separate return electrode. 22. A method as claimed in claim 19, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a bipolar device or a multi phase RF device, wherein said method optionally further comprises providing a separate return electrode or electrodes. 23. A method as claimed in any of claims 19-22, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a rapid evaporation ionization mass spectrometry ("REIMS") device. 24. A method as claimed in any of claims 19-23, wherein the step of automatically sampling a plurality of different locations on said sample further comprises applying an AC or RF voltage to said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips. 25. A method as claimed in claim 24, wherein the step of applying said AC or RF voltage to said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips further comprises applying one or more pulses of said AC or RF voltage to said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips. 26. A method as claimed in claim 24 or 25, wherein said step of applying said AC or RF voltage to said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips causes heat to be dissipated into said sample. 27. A method as claimed in claim 24, 25 or 26, wherein said RF voltage has an amplitude, a peak to peak voltage or a RMS voltage selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 100 V; (ii) about 100-200 V; (iii) about 200-300 V; (iv) about 300-400 V; (v) about 400-500 V; (vi) about 500-600 V; (vii) about 600-700 V; (viii) about 700-800 V; (ix) about 800-900 V; (x) about 900-1000 V; and (xi) > about 1 kV. 28. A method as claimed in any of claims 24-27, wherein said RF voltage has a frequency selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 1 kHz; (ii) about 1-2 kHz; (iii) about 2-3 kHz; (iv) about 3-4 kHz; (v) about 4-5 kHz; (vi) about 5-6 kHz; (vii) about 6-7 kHz; (viii) about 7-8 kHz; (ix) about 8-9 kHz; (x) about 9-10 kHz; (xi) about 10-20 kHz; (xii) about 20-30 kHz; (xiii) about 30-40 kHz; (xiv) about 40-50 kHz; (xv) about 50-60 kHz; (xvi) about 60-70 kHz; (xvii) about 70-80 kHz; (xviii) about 80-90 kHz; (xix) about 90-100 kHz; (xx) about 100-200 kHz; (xxi) about 200-300 kHz; (xxii) about 300-400 kHz; (xxiii) about 400- 500 kHz; (xxiv) about 500-600 kHz; (xxv) about 600-700 kHz; (xxvi) about 700-800 kHz; (xxvii) about 800-900 kHz; (xxviii) about 900-1000 kHz; (xxix) about 1-2 MHz; and (xxx) > about 2 MHz. 29. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device is arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from said sample by direct evaporation or vaporisation of target material from said sample by Joule heating or diathermy. 30. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said aerosol, smoke or vapour comprises uncharged aqueous droplets optionally comprising cellular material. 31. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein at least 50%, 55%, 60%, 65%, 70%, 75%, 80%, 85%, 90% or 95% of the mass or matter generated by said first device and which forms said aerosol is in the form of droplets. 32. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device is arranged and adapted to generate aerosol wherein the Sauter mean diameter ("SMD", d32) of said aerosol is in a range: (i) < 5 Î¼Î·Î¹; (ii) 5-10 Î¼Î·Î¹; (iii) 10-15 Î¼Î·Î¹; (iv) 15-20 Î¼Î·Î¹; (v) 20-25 Î¼Î·Î¹; or (vi) > 25 Î¼Î·Î¹. 33. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said aerosol traverses a flow region with a Reynolds number (Re) in the range: (i) < 2000; (ii) 2000-2500; (iii) 2500-3000; (iv) 3000-3500; (v) 3500-4000; or (vi) > 4000. 34. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein substantially at the point of generating said aerosol, said aerosol comprises droplets having a Weber number (We) selected from the group consisting of: (i) < 50; (ii) 50-100; (iii) 100-150; (iv) 150-200; (v) 200-250;(vi) 250-300; (vii) 300-350; (viii) 350-400; (ix) 400-450; (x) 450-500; (xi) 500-550; (xii) 550-600; (xiii) 600-650; (xiv) 650-700; (xv) 700-750; (xvi) 750-800; (xvii) 800-850; (xviii) 850-900; (xix) 900-950; (xx) 950-1000; and (xxi) > 1000. 35. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein substantially at the point of generating said aerosol, said aerosol comprises droplets having a Stokes number (S k ) in the range: (i) 1-5; (ii) 5-10; (iii) 10-15; (iv) 15-20; (v) 20-25; (vi) 25-30; (vii) 30-35; (viii) 35- 40; (ix) 40-45; (x) 45-50; and (xi) > 50. 36. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein substantially at the point of generating said aerosol, said aerosol comprises droplets having a mean axial velocity selected from the group consisting of: (i) < 20 m/s; (ii) 20-30 m/s; (iii) 30-40 m/s; (iv) 40-50 m/s; (v) 50-60 m/s; (vi) 60-70 m/s; (vii) 70-80 m/s; (viii) 80-90 m/s; (ix) 90-100 m/s; (x) 100- 1 10 m/s; (xi) 1 10-120 m/s; (xii) 120-130 m/s; (xiii) 130-140 m/s; (xiv) 140-150 m/s; and (xv) > 150 m/s. 37. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, wherein said first device comprises a point of care ("POC"), diagnostic or surgical device. 38. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising ionizing at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour so as to generate analyte ions. 39. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising aspirating at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour produced from said sample. 40. A method as claimed in claim 39, further comprising aspirating said aerosol, smoke or vapour in a substantially pulsed, discontinuous or irregular manner. 41. A method as claimed in claim 39 or 40, further comprising aspirating said aerosol, smoke or vapour substantially only when an electrosurgical cutting applied voltage or potential is supplied to one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips. 42. A method as claimed in claim 40 or 41 , further comprising varying an aspiration duty cycle during the course of a surgical, non-surgical or other procedure. 43. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising passing said aerosol, smoke or vapour into a vacuum chamber of a mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer. 44. A method as claimed in claim 43, further comprising ionizing at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour within a or said vacuum chamber of said mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer so as to generate analyte ions. 45. A method as claimed in claim 43 or 44, further comprising causing at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour to impact upon a collision surface located within a vacuum chamber of said mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer so as to generate analyte ions. 46. A method as claimed in claim 45, further comprising heating said collision surface. 47. A method as claimed in claim 46, wherein the step of heating said collision surface comprises heating said collision surface to a temperature selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 100 Â°C; (ii) about 100-200 Â°C; (iii) about 200-300 Â°C; (iv) about 300-400 Â°C; (v) about 400-500 Â°C; (vi) about 500-600 Â°C; (vii) about 600-700 Â°C; (viii) about 700-800 Â°C; (ix) about 800-900 Â°C; (x) about 900-1000 Â°C; (xi) about 1000-1100 Â°C; and (xii) > about 1 100 Â°C. 48. A method as claimed in any of claims 38-47, further comprising mass analysing and/or ion mobility analysing said analyte ions in order to obtain said mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data corresponding to each said location. 49. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising mass analysing and/or ion mobility analysing said aerosol, smoke or vapour or ions derived from said 

 aerosol, smoke or vapour in order to obtain said mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data corresponding to each said location. 50. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising adding a matrix to said aerosol, smoke or vapour. 51. A method as claimed in claim 50, wherein said matrix is added to said aerosol, smoke or vapour prior to said aerosol, smoke or vapour impacting upon said collision surface. 52. A method as claimed in claim 50 or 51 , wherein said matrix is selected from the group consisting of: (i) a solvent for said aerosol, smoke or vapour; (ii) an organic solvent; (iii) a volatile compound; (iv) polar molecules; (v) water; (vi) one or more alcohols; (vii) methanol; (viii) ethanol; (ix) isopropanol; (x) acetone; (xi) acetonitrile; (xii) 1-butanol; (xiii) tetrahydrofuran; (xiv) ethyl acetate; (xv) ethylene glycol; (xvi) dimethyl sulfoxide; (xvii) an aldehyde; (xviii) a ketone; (xiv) non-polar molecules; (xx) hexane; (xxi) chloroform; (xxii) butanol; and (xxiii) propanol . 53. A method as claimed in claim 50, 51 or 52, wherein said matrix comprises a lockmass or calibration compound. 54. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising operating said first device in a cutting mode of operation wherein said first device forms one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 55. A method as claimed in claim 54, further comprising maintaining said first device at substantially the same height over said sample whilst performing said one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 56. A method as claimed in claim 54 or 55, further comprising maintaining said first device in substantially continuous contact with said sample whilst performing said one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 57. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising operating said first device in a pointing mode of operation. 58. A method as claimed in claim 57, further comprising lowering said first device so as to contact said sample and to acquire mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data and then raising said first device after contacting said sample and prior to acquiring further mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data. 59. A method as claimed in any preceding claim, further comprising obtaining an optical image of said sample. 60. A method as claimed in claim 59, further comprising substantially co-registering said optical image and an ion image. 61. A method as claimed in claim 60, further comprising defining one or more regions of interest in said optical image and/or said ion image. 62. A method as claimed in claim 60 or 61 , further comprising determining a class or classification of one or more regions of interest. 63. A method as claimed in claim 62, wherein said class or classification comprises a healthy status, a pre-cancerous status, a cancerous status or a bacterial strain. 64. A method comprising: sampling a plurality of different locations of a sample using a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from the sample to obtain mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data at each said location; and using a sample classification model which was previously constructed, trained or improved according to a method of ion imaging as claimed in any of claims 1-63 in order to classify said sample at each said location. 65. A method of electrosurgery comprising: using one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips to acquire a sample from a plurality of different locations of a biological tissue using a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from the biological tissue; obtaining mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data at each said location; and using a biological tissue classification model which was previously constructed, trained or improved according to a method of ion imaging as claimed in any of claims 1-63 in order to classify said biological tissue at each said location. 66. An ion imager comprising: a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from a sample; and a control system arranged and adapted: (i) to automatically sample a plurality of different locations on said sample using said first device and to obtain mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data corresponding to each said location; and (ii) to use said obtained mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data to construct, train or improve a sample classification model. 67. An ion imager as claimed in claim 66, wherein said sample comprises a biological sample, biological tissue, human tissue, animal tissue, biological matter, a bacterial colony, a fungal colony or one or more bacterial strains. 68. An ion imager as claimed in claim 66 or 67, wherein said sample classification model comprises a biological sample classification model, a biological tissue classification model, a human tissue classification model, an animal tissue classification model or a bacterial strain classification model. 69. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66, 67 or 68, further comprising a device arranged and adapted to automatically translate said sample relative to said first device any one or more of before, during, and after obtaining mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data from at least some of said locations on said sample. 70. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-69, wherein said first device comprises or forms part of an ambient ion or ionization source or wherein said first device generates said aerosol, smoke or vapour for subsequent ionization by an ambient ion or ionization source or other ionization source. 71. An ion image as claimed in any of claims 66-70, wherein said first device comprises an ion source selected from the group consisting of: (i) a rapid evaporative ionization mass spectrometry ("REIMS") ion source; (ii) a desorption electrospray ionization ("DESI") ion source; (iii) a laser desorption ionization ("LDI") ion source; (iv) a thermal desorption ion source; (v) a laser diode thermal desorption ("LDTD") ion source; (vi) a desorption electro- flow focusing ("DEFFI") ion source; (vii) a dielectric barrier discharge ("DBD") plasma ion source; (viii) an Atmospheric Solids Analysis Probe ("ASAP") ion source; (ix) an ultrasonic assisted spray ionization ion source; (x) an easy ambient sonic-spray ionization ("EASI") ion source; (xi) a desorption atmospheric pressure photoionization ("DAPPI") ion source; (xii) a paperspray ("PS") ion source; (xiii) a jet desorption ionization ("JeDI") ion source; (xiv) a touch spray ("TS") ion source; (xv) a nano-DESI ion source; (xvi) a laser ablation electrospray ("LAESI") ion source; (xvii) a direct analysis in real time ("DART") ion source; (xviii) a probe electrospray ionization ("PESI") ion source; (xix) a solid-probe assisted electrospray ionization ("SPA-ESI") ion source; (xx) a cavitron ultrasonic surgical aspirator ("CUSA") device; (xxi) a focussed or unfocussed ultrasonic ablation device; (xxii) a microwave resonance device; and (xxiii) a pulsed plasma RF dissection device. 72. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-71 , wherein said first device comprises one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips and wherein said first device is arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from said sample by contacting said sample with said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips at each said location. 73. An ion imager as claimed in claim 72, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a monopolar device. 74. An ion imager as claimed in claim 73, further comprising a separate return electrode. 75. An ion imager as claimed in claim 72, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a bipolar device or a multi phase RF device, wherein said ion imager optionally further comprises a separate return electrode or electrodes. 76. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 72-75, wherein said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips comprise a rapid evaporation ionization mass spectrometry ("REIMS") device. 77. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 72-76, further comprising a device arranged and adapted to apply an AC or RF voltage to said one or more electrodes or said one or more electrosurgical tips. 78. An ion imager as claimed in claim 77, wherein said RF voltage has an amplitude, a peak to peak voltage or a RMS voltage selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 100 V; (ii) about 100-200 V; (iii) about 200-300 V; (iv) about 300-400 V; (v) about 400-500 V; (vi) about 500-600 V; (vii) about 600-700 V; (viii) about 700-800 V; (ix) about 800-900 V; (x) about 900-1000 V; and (xi) > about 1 kV. 79. An ion imager as claimed in claim 77 or 78, wherein said RF voltage has a frequency selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 1 kHz; (ii) about 1-2 kHz; (iii) about 2-3 kHz; (iv) about 3-4 kHz; (v) about 4-5 kHz; (vi) about 5-6 kHz; (vii) about 6-7 kHz; (viii) about 7-8 kHz; (ix) about 8-9 kHz; (x) about 9-10 kHz; (xi) about 10-20 kHz; (xii) about 20-30 kHz; (xiii) about 30-40 kHz; (xiv) about 40-50 kHz; (xv) about 50-60 kHz; (xvi) about 60-70 kHz; (xvii) about 70-80 kHz; (xviii) about 80-90 kHz; (xix) about 90-100 kHz; (xx) about 100-200 kHz; (xxi) about 200-300 kHz; (xxii) about 300-400 kHz; (xxiii) about 400- 500 kHz; (xxiv) about 500-600 kHz; (xxv) about 600-700 kHz; (xxvi) about 700-800 kHz; (xxvii) about 800-900 kHz; (xxviii) about 900-1000 kHz; (xxix) about 1-2 MHz; and (xxx) > about 2 MHz. 80. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-80, wherein said device is arranged and adapted to aspirate said aerosol, smoke or vapour in a substantially pulsed, discontinuous or irregular manner. 82. An ion imager as claimed in claim 80 or 81 , wherein said device is arranged and adapted to aspirate said aerosol, smoke or vapour substantially only when an electrosurgical cutting applied voltage or potential is supplied to one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips. 83. An ion imager as claimed in claim 81 or 82, further comprising a control system which is arranged and adapted to vary an aspiration duty cycle during the course of a surgical, non-surgical or other procedure. 84. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-83, further comprising a control system which is arranged and adapted to operate said first device in a cutting mode of operation wherein said first device forms one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 85. An ion imager as claimed in claim 84, wherein said control system is further arranged and adapted to maintain said first device at substantially the same height over said sample whilst performing said one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 86. An ion imager as claimed in claim 84 or 85, wherein said control system is further arranged and adapted to maintain said first device in substantially continuous contact with said sample whilst performing said one or more substantially continuous cuts in said sample. 87. An ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-88, further comprising a control system which is arranged and adapted to obtain an optical image of said sample. 90. An ion imager as claimed in claim 89, wherein said control system is further arranged and adapted to substantially co-register said optical image and an ion image. 91. An ion imager as claimed in claim 90, wherein said control system is further arranged and adapted to define one or more regions of interest in said optical image and/or said ion image. 92. An ion imager as claimed in claim 90 or 91 , wherein said control system is further arranged and adapted to determine a class or classification of one or more regions of interest. 93. An ion imager as claimed in claim 92, wherein said class or classification comprises a healthy status, a pre-cancerous status, a cancerous status or a bacterial strain. 94. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer comprising an ion imager as claimed in any of claims 66-93. 95. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 94, further comprising tubing or other means which is arranged and adapted to pass said aerosol, smoke or vapour into a vacuum chamber of said mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer. 96. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 94 or 95, further comprising a collision surface located within a vacuum chamber of said mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer. 97. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 96, wherein in use at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour is caused to impact upon said collision surface. 98. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 97, wherein in use at least some of said aerosol, smoke or vapour is ionized upon impacting said collision surface so as to generate analyte ions. 99. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 96, 97 or 98, further comprising a heater which is arranged and adapted to heat said collision surface. 100. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 99, wherein said heater is arranged and adapted to heat said collision surface to a temperature selected from the group consisting of: (i) < about 100 Â°C; (ii) about 100-200 Â°C; (iii) about 200-300 Â°C; (iv) about 300-400 Â°C; (v) about 400-500 Â°C; (vi) about 500-600 Â°C; (vii) about 600-700 Â°C; (viii) about 700-800 Â°C; (ix) about 800-900 Â°C; (x) about 900-1000 Â°C; (xi) about 1000-1100 Â°C; and (xii) > about 1 100 Â°C. 101. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 98, 99 or 100, further comprising a mass analyser and/or ion mobility analyser for mass analysing and/or ion mobility analysing said analyte ions. 102. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in any of claims 94-101 , further comprising a device which is arranged and adapted to add a matrix to said aerosol, smoke or vapour. 103. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 102, wherein said device is arranged and adapted to add said matrix to said aerosol, smoke or vapour prior to said aerosol, smoke or vapour impacting upon said collision surface. 104. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 102 or 103, wherein said matrix is selected from the group consisting of: (i) a solvent for said aerosol, smoke or vapour; (ii) an organic solvent; (iii) a volatile compound; (iv) polar molecules; (v) water; (vi) one or more alcohols; (vii) methanol; (viii) ethanol; (ix) isopropanol; (x) acetone; and (xi) acetonitrile. 105. A mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer as claimed in claim 102, 103 or 104, wherein said matrix comprises a lockmass or calibration compound. 106. Apparatus comprising: a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from a sample; and a control system arranged and adapted: (i) to sample a plurality of different locations of said sample using said first device and to obtain mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data at each said location; and (ii) to use a sample classification model which was previously constructed, trained or improved according to a method of ion imaging as claimed in any of claims 1-63 in order to classify said sample at each said location. 107. Electrosurgical apparatus comprising: a first device arranged and adapted to generate aerosol, smoke or vapour from a biological tissue comprising one or more electrodes or one or more electrosurgical tips which are arranged and adapted to sample a plurality of different locations of said biological tissue; a mass spectrometer and/or ion mobility spectrometer arranged and adapted to obtain mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data at each said location; and a control system which is arranged and adapted to use a biological tissue classification model which was previously constructed, trained or improved according to a 

 method of ion imaging as claimed in any of claims 1-63 in order to classify said biological tissue at each said location. 108. A method as claimed in any of claims 1-65, wherein using said obtained mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data to construct, train or improve a sample classification model comprises analysing one or more sample spectra of said mass spectral data and/or ion mobility data so as to classify an aerosol, smoke or vapour sample. 109. A method as claimed in claim 108, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises supervised analysis of the one or more sample spectra and/or unsupervised analysis of the one or more sample spectra. 110. A method as claimed in claim 108 or 109, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises using one or more of: univariate analysis; multivariate analysis; principal component analysis (PCA); linear discriminant analysis (LDA); maximum margin criteria (MMC); library-based analysis; soft independent modelling of class analogy (SIMCA); factor analysis (FA); recursive partitioning (decision trees); random forests; independent component analysis (ICA); partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA); orthogonal (partial least squares) projections to latent structures (OPLS); OPLS discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA); support vector machines (SVM); (artificial) neural networks; multilayer perceptron; radial basis function (RBF) networks; Bayesian analysis; cluster analysis; a kernelized method; and subspace discriminant analysis. 11 1. A method as claimed in claim 108, 109 or 110, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises developing a classification model or library using one or more reference sample spectra. 112. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-11 1 , wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises performing linear discriminant analysis (LDA) after performing principal component analysis (PCA). 113. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-11 1 , wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises performing a maximum margin criteria (MMC) process after performing principal component analysis (PCA). 114. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-113, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises defining one or more classes within a classification model or library. 115. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-114, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises defining one or more classes within a classification model or library manually or automatically according to one or more class or cluster criteria. 116. A method as claimed in claim 115, wherein the one or more class or cluster criteria for each class are based on one or more of: a distance between one or more pairs of reference points for reference sample spectra within a model space; a variance value between groups of reference points for reference sample spectra within a model space; and a variance value within a group of reference points for reference sample spectra within a model space. 117. A method as claimed in claim 1 14, 1 15 or 1 16, wherein the one or more classes are each defined by one or more class definitions. 118. A method as claimed in claim 117, wherein the one or more class definitions comprise one or more of: a set of one or more reference points for reference sample spectra, values, boundaries, lines, planes, hyperplanes, variances, volumes, Voronoi cells, and/or positions, within a model space; and one or more positions within a class hierarchy. 119. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-118, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises using a classification model or library to classify one or more unknown sample spectra. 120. A method as claimed in any of claims 108-119, wherein analysing the one or more sample spectra so as to classify the aerosol, smoke or vapour sample comprises classifying one or more sample spectra manually or automatically according to one or more classification criteria. 121. A method as claimed in claim 120, wherein the one or more classification criteria comprise one or more of: a distance between one or more projected sample points for one or more sample spectra within a model space and a set of one or more reference points for one or more reference sample spectra, values, boundaries, lines, planes, hyperplanes, volumes, Voronoi cells, or positions, within the model space being below a distance threshold or being the lowest such distance; a position for one or more projected sample points for one or more sample spectra within a model space being one side or other of one or more reference points for one or more reference sample spectra, values, boundaries, lines, planes, hyperplanes, or positions, within the model space; 

 a position for one or more projected sample points for one or more sample spectra within a model space being within one or more volumes or Voronoi cells within the model space; and a probability or classification score being above a probability or classification score threshold or being the highest such probability or classification score.
